original_abstract:
Liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry (LC-MS) is a cornerstone of modern metabolomics, favored for its high sensitivity and ability to rapidly quantify cellular metabolites. To increase throughput, there is a growing trend toward using minimal or no chromatographic separation. However, this study demonstrates that such approaches risk significant data misinterpretation due to in-source fragmentation. Even when using electrospray ionization (ESI), often considered a "soft" ionization technique, high-energy regions in the mass spectrometer can fragment larger molecules into ions that are indistinguishable from smaller, common metabolites.

By examining the intracellular metabolome of yeast, we identified over 20 instances where in-source fragments mimicked authentic cellular metabolites of lower molecular weight. Notable examples include nucleotide triphosphates (e.g., ATP) fragmenting into their diphosphate forms (e.g., ADP), and hexose phosphates (such as glucose-6-phosphate) generating ions that mimic triose, tetrose, and pentose phosphates. These artifacts often co-elute near the true analytes, leading to substantial misannotation and overestimation of metabolite concentrations. Utilizing isotopic labeling and genetic enzyme knockdowns, we confirmed that several high-priority metabolic intermediates are frequently masked by these fragmentation products. Our findings underscore the necessity of robust chromatographic separation and rigorous annotation protocols—including the use of authentic standards and co-elution analysis—to ensure accurate quantification in LC-MS-based metabolomic studies.
humanized_abstract:
Liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry (LC-MS) is widely used as a platform for metabolomics due to its ability to achieve highly sensitive and rapid metabolite quantification. One strategy to further enhance speed and throughput in sample analysis is to use little to no chromatography, as can be achieved using shotgun approaches. In the current work, it was shown that metabolomics information generated in the absence of chromatography can contain flawed interpretations that can be compromised by up to 75% due to in-source fragmentation of larger analytes to fragmentation products that are indistinguishable from that of smaller, more abundant ions, even under electrospray ionization (ESI) conditions commonly perceived as being soft.

In the context of the intracellular yeast metabolome, we have recently discovered more than 20 cases where in-source fragmentation of higher MW precursor ions results in fragments that mimic authentic cellular metabolites of lower mass. Interestingly, nucleotide triphosphates such as ATP fragmented into their corresponding nucleotide triphosphate products (e.g., ADP) and hexose-phosphates (such as glucose-6-phosphate) fragmented into fragments mimicking triose, tetrose and even pentose-phosphates. These fragmentation products co-elute with their authentic precursor metabolites in nearly all cases, and hence are a major source of contamination and over-estimation of peak intensity which in turn lead to gross over-estimation of metabolite concentrations. Using the strategy of mass labeled samples as well as strain with enzyme knocked-down expression we have confirmed that many key and high priority precursors are highly contaminated by the fragmentation products of higher MW precursor ions. Hence the need for rigorous separations and better validation/ identification of peaks, using authentic standards and co-elution criteria etc, while performing LC-MS analysis for metabolomics studies.

document.output:
Liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry (LC-MS) is widely used as a platform for metabolomics due to its ability to achieve highly sensitive and rapid metabolite quantification. One strategy to further enhance speed and throughput in sample analysis is to use little to no chromatography, as can be achieved using shotgun approaches. In the context of the intracellular yeast metabolome, we have recently discovered more than 20 cases where in-source fragmentation of higher MW precursor ions results in fragments that mimic authentic cellular metabolites of lower mass. Interestingly, nucleotide triphosphates such as ATP fragmented into their corresponding nucleotide triphosphate products (e.g., ADP) and hexose-phosphates (such as glucose-6-phosphate) fragmented into fragments mimicking triose, tetrose and even pentose-phosphates. document.input:
Liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry (LC-MS) is a cornerstone of modern metabolomics, favored for its high sensitivity and ability to rapidly quantify cellular metabolites. To increase throughput, there is a growing trend toward using minimal or no chromatographic separation. However, this study demonstrates that such approaches risk significant data misinterpretation due to in-source fragmentation.
